summary: All 42 taint flows from automated analysis are FALSE POSITIVES. No exploitable unauthenticated vulnerabilities confirmed. The plugin is well-hardened by Automattic's security practices: HMAC-signed endpoints, wp_safe_redirect everywhere, md5 hashing of cache keys, wp_safe_remote_get for outbound requests, and proper capability checks on admin actions.

verdicts.title: Minify service fallback - REQUEST_URI flows
verdicts.reasoning: REQUEST_URI is passed through md5() before being used as a cache filename, making path traversal impossible. Cache content is server-generated CSS/JS, not attacker-controlled. Content-Type headers are set from server-stored metadata. No XSS, no file inclusion, no open redirect.

verdicts.title: preg_replace RCE in minify service
verdicts.reasoning: The preg_replace uses /isU flags, NOT /e modifier. The /e modifier (eval-based RCE) was removed in PHP 7.0. The tainted $dirpath appears in the replacement string only, not the pattern. No RCE possible.

verdicts.title: Module disable via GET parameter
verdicts.reasoning: The $_GET value is passed through sanitize_key() which strips everything except [a-z0-9_-]. The result is used only as an array key for in_array comparison against known module slugs. No code execution path exists.

verdicts.title: Image Guide proxy SSRF/XSS
verdicts.reasoning: Handler registered via wp_ajax_ (NOT wp_ajax_nopriv_), requires manage_options capability, and verifies nonce. URL is rewritten to Photon CDN domain before fetch. wp_safe_remote_get blocks internal IP targets. Response body is never echoed (only headers returned as JSON). Triple-protected: auth + nonce + CDN rewrite.

verdicts.title: Critical CSS proxy SSRF/XSS
verdicts.reasoning: Same triple protection as image guide proxy: wp_ajax_ only (admin auth required), nonce verified, wp_safe_remote_get used. URL must end in .css and response Content-Type must contain text/css. Output uses wp_strip_all_tags which prevents style block breakout. Admin-only, not exploitable by unauthenticated users.

verdicts.title: Jetpack Sync dedicated sender SSRF
verdicts.reasoning: The $url is constructed from rest_url() (site's own URL), not from user input. $_COOKIE is passed as the cookies HTTP argument (forwarded as cookies to the loopback request), not as the URL target. This is a standard WordPress loopback request pattern. No SSRF: attacker cannot control the destination.

verdicts.title: Jetpack Sync module SQL/unserialize
verdicts.reasoning: The flagged code is an internal database query using wp_parse_id_list() (which casts all values to integers) and a server-side whitelist of meta keys. The result flows through unserialize_meta which is processing database-stored values, not direct user input. The tool misidentified array_map as an RCE sink.

verdicts.title: Plugin/theme file editor write
verdicts.reasoning: Both flows are in Jetpack Sync modules that OBSERVE WordPress core's plugin/theme editor actions. auth_required=true, nonce_verified=true. These hooks fire AFTER WordPress core has already validated the file write (which requires edit_plugins/edit_themes capability). The Sync module is recording what happened, not initiating the write.

verdicts.title: JITM query string / REQUEST_URI echo
verdicts.reasoning: auth_required=true (admin context). The output uses urlencode_deep() which URL-encodes special characters including < > " preventing XSS. Additionally, JITM is rendered in WordPress admin only (requires authentication).

verdicts.title: Object injection via maybe_unserialize on options
verdicts.reasoning: maybe_unserialize on get_option() is standard WordPress practice. The option values are stored by the plugin itself or by WordPress core. An attacker would need direct database write access to inject a malicious serialized payload, at which point they already have full site compromise. Not an independent vulnerability.

verdicts.title: Variable injection via parse_str in Jetpack Signature
verdicts.reasoning: parse_str() is called with a second argument ($body), writing into a local variable, NOT into the global symbol table. This is part of HMAC signature verification - if the attacker modifies the body, the signature check fails. The parse_str call is safe by design.

verdicts.title: Licensing endpoints - array_map/array_filter as RCE sinks
verdicts.reasoning: The tool misidentified array_map('strval'), array_filter('is_scalar'), and array_map(sanitize_text_field) as RCE sinks because escapeshellarg() was not used. These are data transformation functions, not shell execution. The license values go through sanitize_text_field and are stored as WordPress options. No shell execution anywhere in the chain.

verdicts.title: My Jetpack initializer XSS via $_GET['step']
verdicts.reasoning: The code does $step === 'onboarding' (strict equality), producing a boolean. The output is a hardcoded string literal 'data-route="onboarding"' or empty string. The user-supplied value is NEVER echoed. Additionally, this runs in wp-admin (admin_menu hook) requiring authentication.

verdicts.title: Connection notice - $_REQUEST['users'] RCE
verdicts.reasoning: Values go through sanitize_text_field then absint() (cast to non-negative integer). Result is used only in in_array() comparison. Runs in admin_notices context (requires wp-admin authentication). The tool misidentified array_map('absint') as an RCE sink.

verdicts.title: Tracking data via $_REQUEST['tracksEventProp']
verdicts.reasoning: nonce_verified=true. Values go through filter_var() and are passed to Tracks analytics (WordPress.com telemetry). No code execution, no database write with unsanitized data. Admin context.

verdicts.title: Open redirect via webhooks class
verdicts.reasoning: All flows use wp_safe_redirect() which calls wp_validate_redirect() internally, restricting redirects to the site's own host plus allowed_redirect_hosts filter (which only adds *.wordpress.com). Cannot redirect to attacker-controlled external domains. The 'from' parameter is only appended as a query parameter value, not as a redirect target.

verdicts.title: Nonce handler - array_filter on DB results
verdicts.reasoning: The code retrieves option_ids from the database using a prepared query, then filters them with array_filter(). These are integer IDs from the options table used for cleanup of expired Jetpack nonces. No user input, no code execution. Tool misidentified array_filter as RCE sink.

verdicts.title: SSO User Admin - intval user IDs
verdicts.reasoning: User input goes through intval() immediately (line 682). Result is an integer used with get_user_by('id'). nonce_verified=true. array_filter and array_map on integer user IDs are not RCE sinks.

verdicts.title: SSO force_reauth open redirect
verdicts.reasoning: $reauth = !empty($_GET['force_reauth']) produces a boolean (true/false). This boolean is passed to get_sso_url_or_die() which constructs a URL to WordPress.com SSO. The redirect target is WordPress.com (allowed host), not attacker-controlled. wp_safe_redirect validates the host.

verdicts.title: SSO cookie-based redirect
verdicts.reasoning: auth_required=true. Cookie value goes through esc_url_raw(). Redirect targets either use admin_url() as base (same-site) or go through wp_safe_redirect + wp_validate_redirect. Attacker-set cookies cannot force redirect to external domains.

verdicts.title: SSO redirect_to via GET parameter
verdicts.reasoning: The redirect_to value goes through: esc_url_raw() -> login_redirect filter -> wp_sanitize_redirect() -> wp_validate_redirect(with admin_url() fallback) -> build_authorize_url() -> wp_safe_redirect(). Five layers of redirect validation. Cannot redirect to external attacker domains.

verdicts.title: Authorize redirect via dest_url GET parameter
verdicts.reasoning: dest_url goes through esc_url_raw() then wp_safe_redirect(). wp_safe_redirect restricts to same-host or allowed_redirect_hosts. Cannot redirect to arbitrary external domains.

informational_findings.title: Cloud CSS stored without content sanitization
informational_findings.description: The cloud-css/update REST endpoint (protected by Signed_With_Blog_Token HMAC auth) stores CSS content from Jetpack cloud servers without sanitization. The CSS is later output via wp_strip_all_tags() in a <style> block. While wp_strip_all_tags prevents HTML tag injection, CSS-level attacks (expression(), url() data exfil) may work in older browsers. A compromise of Jetpack's cloud infrastructure could push malicious CSS to all connected sites. This is a supply-chain trust boundary issue, not an exploitable vulnerability from the internet.

informational_findings.title: Unauthenticated spawn-sync endpoint (DoS potential)
informational_findings.description: GET /wp-json/jetpack/v4/sync/spawn-sync has permission_callback __return_true but the handler is effectively a no-op (returns WP_Error immediately in both enabled/disabled states). Each request costs one DB option read. No meaningful DoS beyond standard WordPress REST API volume. Not a vulnerability.
